title: Spotify OAuth callback is not bound to a caller session and exposes the exchanged refresh token in the response
reasoning: The callback accepts any `code` query parameter, performs the token exchange immediately, and returns the resulting `refresh_token` in the HTTP response without validating an OAuth `state` value or checking that the request is tied to the initiator of the flow. That makes the endpoint vulnerable to OAuth CSRF/account-mixup and turns a one-time authorization code into a bearer-secret disclosure endpoint for whoever can hit the callback URL with that code first.
recommendation: Generate and store a cryptographically random `state` value before starting the Spotify authorization flow, require the callback to validate it, and avoid returning the refresh token in the browser response. Persist the token server-side or show only a success page after securely storing it.
whyTestsDoNotAlreadyCoverThis: No tests are included for the callback route, so there is nothing asserting `state` validation or preventing refresh-token disclosure behavior.
suggestedRegressionTest: Add a route test that rejects callbacks with a missing or mismatched `state`, and another that verifies successful callbacks do not include `refresh_token` in the JSON body returned to the browser.
